background.name: Barber-Surgeon
entries: People on the borderlands are hardy, and many are subject to life in harsh conditions. Barber-surgeons, with some glee, saw off limbs, drill holes in skulls to relieve hematomas, and use medications that are almost as deadly as the injuries and diseases they are trying to cure.

entries.name: Feature: Is It Safe?
entries: Sometimes a limb just needs to be removed or a fever is best dealt with using a lot of leeches. Your methods typically do harm before the patient recovers. You have advantage on Wisdom (Medicine) checks made to stabilize a creature. In addition, when you stabilize a creature, you can remove any severe injury the creature has sustained. Any creature you stabilize regains {@dice 1d6} hit points but has its hit point maximum reduced by {@dice 1d4}. This reduction lasts until the creature finishes a long rest.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: A good job done is something to be enjoyed.
I have a terrible habit of regaling others about surgeries that went wrong.
I've seen a lot of bloodshed, so it takes a lot to shock me.
There's nothing like a good song to lift me up.
A well-prepared satchel of remedies and tools allows me to save as many lives as possible.
Every death is just something destined to happen.
Even saving just one life can make a difference.
The mortal body is fascinating.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Torture. Pain is life, and your life is in my hands. (Evil)
Fate. Life or death, I get a chance to try out new techniques. (Chaotic)
Savior. No one will die on me. (Good)
Healer. I save lives even if they belong to my enemies. (Lawful)
Loyalty. The company I belong to is like family, and I do my duty. (Neutral)
Judgment. Some lives don't deserve a second chance. (Evil)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I have saved many of my company, and they would do anything for me.
I let a person die because I was intoxicated.
In my possession is the badge of a hero who died in my arms.
I brought a child into this world, and I hope to see them grow up.
An enemy slew my mentor on the field of battle, and I will have my revenge.
The reference books on medicine in my possession come from the University of Caspia.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I drink to dispel my demons.
I rush surgeries and have left tools in the patient.
Pride in my work has led to fist fights when my skill has been questioned.
I've allowed people to die when they could not afford treatment.
Deserting my duty has led to people losing their lives.
Everything needs to be in its place or else I can't concentrate.

background.name: Desert Guide

_: desert goggles and a dust mask

entries: {@b Weapon Proficiencies:} Shortbows, spears, simple rifles, martial rifles.

Some, such as the Idrians, various tribes of desert-dwelling bogrin, and trollkin, know the Bloodstone Marches intimately. Those with such skills travel far and wide and live for extended periods in the desert, making them desirable as guides for caravans and armies traversing the scorching plains.

entries.name: Feature: Badlands Explorer
entries: You were brought up in the harsh and hostile environment of the Bloodstone Marches. Desert living is second nature to you, and coping with the baking heat, lack of water, and scouring winds is inherent to your upbringing. You have advantage on Constitution checks made to resist heat or bright light and Wisdom (Survival) checks made to find a water source.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: The sun cleanses everyone, regardless of their faith.
My anger is like the constant thunder of the Bloodstone Marches.
Eventually, everything becomes sand and dust under the scorching winds.
By understanding a new language, you know how a society thinks and feels.
When Calder is bright, I recite the songs of my people.
Hunting big game gives me a thrill.
Under the tribal elders, I have learned all the names of the beasts that roam the deserts.
I will be like the desert wind: quiet but deadly. Like the shadows cast by the midday sun, I am unseen but ready to appear again.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Nature. All we do is part of the cycle of nature. (Lawful)
Self-Sufficiency. If you fall, you are a danger to others. We must stand on our own feet. (Chaotic)
Precision. Even the biggest beast can be felled by a splinter. (Any)
Glory. Others will know my prowess as a hunter of unequaled skill. (Any)
Exploration. I am driven to seek out new horizons and search out the clues that may aid my people. (Neutral)
Community. We must help each other through hardship. (Good)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: This tooth from a crag lion was taken from my first kill.
My sibling lived among the people of the Protectorate of Menoth and I have not seen them since the Claiming.
I have discovered cave paintings made by my people thousands of years ago that tell of the first hunt.
I took my weapon from the grip of a soldier whom I was leading through the desert. That soldier was in pain, and I eased their passing.
I carry a token that has been passed down through the tribe and is important to our rites and prayers.
The song I sing when I am down was taught to me by my mother.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I don't take other people's feelings into consideration.
Justice must always be pursued.
I feel I am closer to the gods than others.
I only trust my own instincts over all else.
Maintaining my weapons uses up all my focus.
Some foods don't agree with me well.

background.name: Fennblade
entries: {@b Weapon Proficiencies:} Greatswords.

Marching forward, swinging massive cleaving blades in wide arcs, Fennblades are a force of nature that hacks through soldiers and warjacks alike. The fighting art has been adopted by kriels across western Immoren.

entries.name: Feature: Drive Them Back
entries: You have been trained to fight shoulder to shoulder with your kin. When an enemy attack damages one or more friendly creatures within 10 feet of you, you can use your reaction to move up to 15 feet toward the attacker and make a single melee attack. Once you use this feature, you can't use it again until you finish a short or long rest.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I stand firm in the face of danger, even if it means more harm comes to me.
Some compare me to a raging dire troll on the field of battle.
Blades do not lie, and neither do I.
Showing others compassion means a stronger family.
I march on my stomach, and hunger turns me sour.
I honor the ancestors and my fallen comrades who shall never be forgotten.
I will laugh and sing the old songs, even as the world comes to an end.
People tend to view me as rough and without manners.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Might. On the battlefield, others will witness my strength. (Any)
Comradeship. Only by standing together can we win. (Good)
Guardian. Fighting means more than victory—it means survival of my kin. (Good)
Brute Force. Only through crushing my enemies do I feel alive. (Evil)
Vendetta. Any who taint the ancestral homelands must be cleaved in two. (Evil)
Faith. Dhunia has a destiny for us all in the cycle of nature. (Neutral)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: Corvis, while a human city, is not without its delights.
My family harbors a secret that would tear the kriel apart.
I am hunted by a family of Khadoran hunters because I killed one of their own for threatening a trollkin child.
During the Claiming, I lost a family heirloom, and I will travel to find it when possible.
I helped to found the kriel and have lived here for many years.
A fellow trollkin in the Cygnaran army is a friendly rival, and we have faced each other a few times on the field of battle.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I hope to take a seat on the council of my kriel.
Never have I run from a fight, and that often gets me into trouble.
Calling out liars to their face is important to me.
Money never lasts me long, as I buy too much food and drink.
It takes my friends' tremendous effort and calming words to break me out of a mindless rage.
North, south, east, west—these are no help to me, as I lose my bearings all the time.

background.name: Guttersnipe

_: a disguise kit or thieves' tools

entries: {@b Weapon Proficiencies:} Daggers.

Living within the cities of the Iron Kingdoms can be difficult for those outside the dominant culture. Gobbers and some of the bogrin who try to make a living in these cities are often pushed to the fringes, where they refine their talents to excel at burglary, espionage, and shadowing others.

entries.name: Feature: Contortionist
entries: You are slim, gangly, or double-jointed and can squeeze through narrow gaps with ease. You don't have disadvantage on attack rolls or Dexterity saving throws while squeezing, and squeezing doesn't cost you extra movement. You also have advantage on checks to wriggle free of bonds and restraints.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: If it isn't nailed down, it's mine.
I have a little rhyme I like to repeat to myself when I pilfer unattended coins.
Everyone has a tale to tell, and those stories are worth money to the right person.
I'm just doing what you don't have the guts to do for yourself.
Some people deserve to be hoodwinked.
I'll score big one day and leave this stinking city behind.
I try to give back to my people because if we can't trust each other, who can we trust?
Often the best thing to do is sell stuff back to the very people you have stolen it from.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Self-Possession. Take what you can, give nothing back. (Chaotic)
Survival. Better them than me. (Evil)
Family. You can always rely on family, and they rely on you. (Lawful)
Daring. You must take chances to score big. (Chaotic)
Trickster. Speak fast enough, distract them, and they will never realize what they are missing. (Neutral)
Honor. I might be a thief, but I'm not a killer. (Good)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I carry a pocket watch I stole from a famous actor.
Under the layers of rags, a pendant made by a sibling out of scrap hangs around my neck.
The home where my fraternity makes camp is deep within the Undercity.
I keep a scratched gold crown at the bottom of my pack. It has always brought me luck.
An alchemist is hunting me down for the schematics I stole.
I'm loyal to the fence who knows how to quickly get a reasonable price for what I lift.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I whistle the same tune to amuse myself.
I have a nervous twitch in one eye.
I lie, even to my own family.
Sometimes I don't know when to shut my mouth, especially when I am with someone I dislike.
I always want a second helping of food.
I don't take lightly being made a fool of.

background.name: Northkin Fire Eaters
entries: Trollkin who make their homes far to the north of Khador ferment potent liquors that warm the body and chase away the chills. These trollkin both drink this alcohol and weaponize it, spitting gouts of the volatile liquids at enemies while igniting it.

entries.name: Feature: Fire Breathing
entries: Each keg of liquor has 3 charges. Drinking from a keg takes an action and expends 1 charge. After drinking, you can spray forth the alcohol and ignite it. As a bonus action, you can exhale fire at a target within 10 feet of you that you can see. The target must make a Dexterity saving throw with a DC of 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Constitution modifier. A creature takes {@damage 2d6} fire damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one. You can't exhale fire unless you have a lit torch in hand or another source of open flame. This damage increases by {@dice 1d6} at 5th level, 10th level, and 15th level.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I love good food and show my appreciation with bellowing belches.
I have on my person a salted fungus that tastes of licorice and that others can smell if I am near.
Studying the types of trees around me and learning which birds make homes in those trees is how I stay calm.
I love a good arm-wrestling.
I enjoy drinking from a horn passed down within my family.
I can't help but tell exaggerated tales of my victories when sitting around a campfire.
I am proud of my brews and don't take criticism well.
I sing, even if I am told I am tone deaf.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Profession. I love my job, and I enjoy others enjoying my work. (Any)
Respect. If you honor me and my kin, I will return the favor. (Neutral)
Fun Loving. Drink and song are what fills life with meaning. (Chaotic)
Brute Force. I am bigger and tougher, and that makes what I say law. (Evil)
Experimentation. Sometimes inspiration and taking risks is how you create new things. (Chaotic)
Kinship. Threaten my family, my kriel, and you threaten me. (Good)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I may have moved to another kriel, but I still miss my family in the north.
I got very, very drunk in Corvis, and I am banned from the city limits.
A rival blamed me for an accident and fled after I revealed them as a liar.
A cousin is the warchief of a related kriel and is seeking to destroy his enemies.
My alcohol was presented to a chieftain, who still speaks of it with pride.
I've a reputation as being a pyromaniac.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I have a short temper and will take to smashing things up around me.
I'm clumsy, even for a trollkin.
Everyone comments on how lazy I am. They are right.
I don't follow orders, which gets me and my friends into trouble.
I won't back down from a challenge, no matter the risk.
If you can't hold your liquor, I won't show you respect.

background.name: Searforge Trader
entries: Throughout western Immoren, some of the finest weapons and sturdiest armor can be bought from Rhulic traders. These traders are savvy merchants with an eye for details and minds for politics, since what they sell can turn the tide of a battle elsewhere in the kingdoms.

entries.name: Feature: Savvy Magnate
entries: Just by ringing a sword or testing the weight of a coin, you can tell the quality, determine the value, and figure out whether someone is trying to pull a fast one on you. You have advantage on Intelligence checks when determining the true value of an item or picking out the best item from a collection. You can requisition passage with a fellow member of the Searforge Commission in exchange for work or information about trade in the region or city you are traveling to. At the GM's discretion, at the start of a significant journey, you can purchase any one type of basic weapon or alchemical resource to sell for a profit at a known destination. The profit margin is determined by the GM.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: Every city I visit is a new adventure. I enjoy learning about the local history and seeing the sights.
No one will ever stiff me on a deal. I always get what I paid for.
I always take the time to compliment people on their craftsmanship.
There is always some new local delicacy to try out.
In my travels, I calm my nerves by singing the traditional songs of my clan, which tell the tales of the history of my ancestors.
I have a collection of trinkets I have picked up over my many journeys.
I like to be direct and straight to the point, to the extent that others think I am rude.
Money means more than feelings, and I often don't care about the impact of the deals I make, so long as they benefit me first.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Faith. I follow the precedence as recorded in the Codex of the Great Fathers. (Lawful)
Daring. Taking risks and investing in opportunities lead to a lot of profit. (Chaotic)
Investor. Good people and good businesses need to be supported to reap benefits in the future. (Good)
Vulture. Some businesses need to be stripped of every asset they have. (Evil)
Knowledge. Only by documenting all things can you best understand the world. (Neutral)
Innovation. Opportunities and industry must be nurtured. (Neutral)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I wear a family signet ring that I use to seal contracts.
I'm on the lookout for a rival who cheated me out of a lucrative contract.
I keep the gold crown from the first sale I ever made in a place of honor.
My sibling is also a trader, and sometimes our paths cross in major cities.
I have invested in a small business that produces small arms. I am building a market for weapons in other territories.
Honoring the clan is the most important thing to me.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I break out in sweats at the sight of blood.
If I can lounge about and still make money, then all the better.
I have nightmares every night about the horrors I witnessed during the Claiming.
My crew must work to the best of their abilities, and I won't stand for any mistakes.
Everyone can be bribed, and I don't trust those who claim otherwise.
I'm a violent drunk. And I often drink, especially after sealing a significant contract.

background.name: Stormfall Archer

_: a pendant featuring the emblem of the Retribution of Scyrah

entries: {@b Weapon Proficiencies:} Martial ranged weapons, shortswords.

The powerful bows used by the Iosan Stormfall archers launch arrows high into the air. When the arrows return, they detonate on impact. Such archers combine the destructive might of cannons and rockets with the mobility of skirmishers.

entries.name: Feature: Bombardment
entries: Stormfall archers can draw back powerful compound bows and launch explosive-tipped arrows of considerable mass. Because of the strength and training required to perform such a feat, you double the normal range of any bow you use.

entries.name: Feature: Skirmishers
entries: After you make a ranged attack, you can use your reaction to move up to half your speed without provoking opportunity attacks.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: A well-executed plan is critical to success.
I watch emotionlessly as my arrows obliterate my enemies from afar.
Victory requires committing to your orders, no matter the risk.
I must stay at the top of my training to best support my people.
Elders and superiors must always be shown respect.
I am at home in the wilderness.
I assume nothing, and I prepare for the worst.
I don't let people get close to me for fear of losing them.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Honesty. I speak my mind and do not lie. (Lawful)
Destruction. I send my enemies running for cover. (Chaotic)
Quiet. Only those who have something to hide speak the loudest. (Neutral)
Immolation. My enemies will be purged by fire and obliterated. (Evil)
Exploration. I am driven to seek out new horizons and search out the clues that may aid my people. (Neutral)
Charity. There are those in need, and I do what I can to help. (Good)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: My bow has been passed down through my family.
I have a sibling who has been missing since the Claiming, and I know they did not return to our homelands.
I carry a dog-eared Iosan novel that I read whenever I have the chance, and I take comfort from its familiar passages.
A rival of mine is a Nyss, and we compete to see who is the best archer.
My unit was destroyed in battle, and I am the last of them. I must live for all of them in these uncertain times.
A skorne warlock seeks revenge on me for killing his prize warbeast.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I solve even the most delicate things without any form of subtlety.
I am driven by the fear that my people are destined for extinction.
I approach every task with precision, no matter how small.
Traitors cannot be abided.
Memories of Ios distract me often.
Jokes often go over my head.

background.name: Urban Nomad

_: a ring made by a jeweler from your homeland

entries: {@b Weapon Proficiencies:} Simple ranged weapons, shortswords.

Like the Nyss, the Iosans are a people without a homeland. Living within the cities of the Iron Kingdoms requires these nomads to adjust to their new lives and environs, as they cope with culture shock, exclusion, and distrust. However, nomads often have particular skills and knowledge that are rare to find within the Iron Kingdoms.

entries.name: Feature: Free Runner
entries: The cities of the Iron Kingdoms are cramped, full of tall buildings, and heaving with people. Rather than just walk the streets, you often take to the rooftops, navigating the city with dexterous, death-defying leaps. You can move through non-magical difficult terrain in urban environments provided it is made up of crowds, rubble, structures, and similar items.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: There are so many interesting foods to try in the cities of Cygnar.
Sometimes I just want to sit and watch the world go by.
I amuse myself reading the lurid tales of city life. Hardly the prose of my homeland, but amusing nonetheless.
I try as much as I can to blend in and not betray my heritage, even adopting much of the local slang.
When I meet others like myself, I try my best to be as friendly as I can.
I always have an escape route planned.
I trust only myself in these strange cities.
I love to discover the hidden artistic gems of the city.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Insight. I cannot help but piece together puzzles and mysteries. (Neutral)
Cutthroat. My enemies will never see me coming. (Evil)
Self-Sufficiency. I have survived alone in the city so long that I can't rely on others. (Chaotic)
Homeland. My lands are barred to me, and so I must make a home here. (Any)
Privacy. Few know my true name, where I am from, or where I sleep at night. (Chaotic)
Daredevil. Sometimes you must just leap and have faith. (Neutral)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: I have fallen in love with someone in this strange city, and I fear if I commit that I will lose a sense of where I came from.
To secure my future, I must score big for my gang.
Enemies from my past hunt me within the city.
A noble from whom I stole a painting wants it back and is trying to have me imprisoned.
There is a ghetto with more of my people living there, and I try to give them food and money when I can.
Each night I gaze out from the rooftop. Through one window, I watch a local family sitting down together for a meal.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I have a habit of leaving without paying.
My sense of humor is not the same as that of the people of this kingdom.
I end up insulting people by simply telling the truth.
I can't swim, and so I panic if I am in a body of water.
It takes me a long time to calm down after being angered.
I forgive no one for their transgressions.

background.name: Wasteland Commando

_: clothing cobbled together from bits and pieces you have recovered
a trophy weapon from an enemy (a dagger or a broken pistol)

entries: {@b Weapon Proficiencies:} Simple rifles, martial rifles, grenades.

Among the Farrow raiding parties, skorne remnants, and Idrian tribes of the Bloodstone Marches are a breed of cunning and murderous commandos. These commandos are not just mindless savages or opportunistic bandits but are calculating tacticians, planning attacks deep behind enemy lines or laying punishing ambushes.

entries.name: Feature: Guerrilla Tactics
entries: You sow chaos and destruction deep behind enemy lines as you destroy ammo caches and fuel depots and sabotage equipment and weapons. You gain a +2 bonus on initiative rolls, and you can't be surprised by humanoids in wilderness environments.

entries.caption: Personality Trait
rows: I take what I want and damn the consequences.
Amongst the tribe, I am a perfect specimen.
I enjoy trying new dishes using new meats and vegetables.
I take great efforts to maintain the firearms and weapons I recover.
My snoring keeps up the camp, and I could sleep through a war.
I get so excited that I can't sit still.
You can find me just sitting and watching the world go by.
I enjoy winding up others with my pranks.

entries.caption: Ideal
rows: Wrath. I will slaughter those who get in my way. (Evil)
Anarchy. Lawlessness will reign thanks to me. (Chaotic)
Loyalty. Traitors to the tribe must be dealt with harshly. (Lawful)
Opportunity. A good fight is always worth it. Especially if you get some loot. (Chaotic)
Oathbound. I uphold a bond to another, just as I expect them to uphold their end of the bargain. (Lawful)
Force of Nature. I look to the Wurm and its myths for direction in life. (Neutral)

entries.caption: Bond
rows: The ornate blade I carry was recovered during a momentous victory.
I was exiled from my old tribe for making a grievous error.
My crew and I are bound by a blood oath that we have upheld for years.
I desire to be respected by my tribe for my martial skills.
Revenge for my murdered sibling drives me.
A relic from my family was stolen, and I have made it my life goal to recover it and so honor my family.

entries.caption: Flaw
rows: I flee the moment a fight is not in my favor.
When it comes to food, I come first.
I put my foot in my mouth all the time.
Coin is all that matters to me.
I have a bad habit of misplacing things.
If I get woken up too soon, I will lash out.

background.name: Badlands Raiders
entries: {@b Adventuring Company.} A traveling caravan of traders has much to fear in their journeys. These brutal parties of opportunistic marauders stalk their quarry and launch surprise attacks that use the landscape to their advantage.

entries.name: Prerequisites
entries: Any character can belong to a Badlands Raiders company, but it must include at least one character proficient in Survival.

entries.name: This Is a Stick Up
entries: As a downtime activity, any number of members from your company can set out to ambush travelers and locals on the roads. Each member chooses and rolls a {@dice d4}, {@dice d6}, {@dice d8}, or {@dice d10}. Each roll of 1 and each roll greater than the character's Wisdom modifier + proficiency bonus is a failure. The company gains 10 gp × the sum of the successful rolls. If the total number of failures is greater than the maximum Wisdom modifier + proficiency bonus among all the company's members, the raid results in a conflict with a well-armed force consisting of {@dice 3d4} creatures with a challenge rating of 1.

entries.name: Company Accomplishments
entries: Your company's prestige increases through acts such as: hijacking a caravan, train, or steamboat; destroying a rival tribe or company of raiders; breaking someone out of prison; stealing an item worth at least 1,000 gp; capturing and selling 1,000 gp worth of livestock.

entries.name: Tier 1 — Cattle Rustlers
entries: {@b Hideout.} You maintain a small camp somewhere in the scrubland or in a cave in the shadow of the mountains. Here you tend to whatever steeds and animals you have captured. You have tents for sleeping and a place where you stash your ill-gotten gains.

{@b Lay of the Land.} You can scout the lands around you and gather information from other locals and travelers about potential marks. Spending downtime to gather such information requires an expenditure of {@dice 1d4} gp, which goes toward bribes and drinks. Gathering information takes {@dice 1d4} days.

{@b Martial Hand Signals.} You can learn martial hand signals, which allows you and other members of your company to communicate silently, thereby allowing you to plan attacks without revealing your position via speech.

entries.name: Tier 2 — Highway Bandits
entries: {@b Bandits.} Novices and new recruits seek out your group to learn your ways and make a life for themselves. You gain {@dice 1d4} hirelings with a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower.
{@b Improved Steeds.} While you are riding your steed, attack rolls targeting your mount are made with disadvantage.
{@b Loyal Informants.} You now have leads on targets carrying 200 gp of valuables or the movement of herds of an equivalent value.

entries.name: Tier 3 — Feared Raiders
entries: {@b Additional Camps.} Your company maintains {@dice 1d4} extra campsites where supplies are stashed in case you need to flee your regular camp.
{@b Bandit Kings.} Your company gains {@dice 2d8} hirelings with a challenge rating of 1/2 or lower. Half these new recruits have mounts with a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower.

{@b Battle Steeds.} Your steeds and mounts have been trained to deal lethal attacks with their hooves, claws, and tusks. As a bonus action, you can direct your mount to make one attack with one of its weapons.

entries.name: Tier 4 — Lords of the Badlands
entries: {@b Hardened Killers.} The number of hirelings you can have increases by {@dice 2d4}, and your hirelings can have a challenge rating of 1 or lower. These new recruits ride on mounts with a challenge rating of 1/2 or lower.
{@b Tithes.} You claim a tithe of 200 gp a month from groups of raiders engaging in smaller actions.

entries.name: Quirks
entries: Roll or choose a quirk for your company: (1) Your company will only raid merchants on certain trade routes and protects those farms that fall within your territory; (2) Members have alternate identities as simple, law-abiding farmers; (3) Your gang is part of a larger organization of raiders, and you must send tithes to your superiors; (4) Your main hideout is hidden in an old mine, a farmstead, or an old temple; (5) Your founding members are all veterans of a conflict in which your tribes were destroyed; (6) Your company robs from the rich and gives to the poor; (7) Your company makes camp in a village that has been devoid of life since the Claiming; (8) You dress up like creatures of myth and folklore when you ride out; (9) Your company includes former tribal leaders who were ousted from their positions; (10) Your company's original founder languishes in some mine or quarry.

background.name: Caravanners of the Iron Roads
entries: {@b Adventuring Company.} Some claim neither Iron Kingdom citizenship nor tribal kinship. These extended families travel the length and breadth of western Immoren, transporting cargo and goods to settlements not easily reached by river or by railroad. These trade routes are known as the Iron Roads.

entries.name: Prerequisites
entries: Anyone can be a member of a Caravanners of the Iron Roads company, as a multitude of skills are required to travel the Iron Roads.

entries.name: Company Accomplishments
entries: Your company's prestige increases through acts such as: establishing a new trade contract worth at least 500 gp; establishing a new trade route; putting a rival caravan out of business; trading a single item for at least 1,000 gp; defeating a much-reviled band of brigands.

entries.name: Tier 1 — Ostlers
entries: {@b Caravan.} You have {@dice 2d3} wagons large enough to carry goods or a single laborjack, with enough draft animals to pull them. Alternatively, you have {@dice 3d6} mules and draft animals to carry your cargo.

{@b Iron Road Tongue.} You can replace one language you gain from your race or background with Iron Road Tongue. You learn Iron Road Cant, which you can use to pass along secret messages by speaking in code or carving runes. Deciphering the cant requires tools or magic (DC 15 Wisdom (Perception) to notice it).
{@b Life in the Saddle.} You can replace one proficiency you gain from your race or background with proficiency in the Animal Handling skill or the Persuasion skill.

entries.name: Tier 2 — Seasoned Traders
entries: {@b Laborers.} You gain your choice of {@dice 2d4} hirelings with a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower or a clapped-out steamjack and a carriage with fuel for it.
{@b Regular Income.} You can claim a steady income of 100 gp after taxes and can buy new stock to trade at the next town.

entries.name: Tier 3 — Merchants of the Iron Roads
entries: {@b Caravanserai.} You know the location of {@dice 1d4} caravanserai—places along the main trade routes that provide stables, accommodations, and protection. You can easily take long rests at these locations and can withdraw money owed to you or take out up to 200 gp in credit.
{@b Heavy Metal Merchants.} You gain a light laborjack—a Forager class 'jack with a cupernum-grade cortex.

entries.name: Tier 4 — Grand Caravan
entries: {@b Greasing the Gears.} You gain a +2 bonus on Wisdom (Insight) checks and Charisma (Persuasion) checks.
{@b Money Makes the World Go 'Round.} You have a regular monthly income after taxes of 500 gp.

entries.name: Quirks
entries: Roll or choose a quirk: (1) Your company crest is that of a fallen noble family; (2) Your caravan sings ancient songs from the time of the Molgur tribes; (3) Your caravan is part of a more prominent extended family that holds a great festival at the summer solstice; (4) The caravan is hunted by a crime lord from Khador for an item a thief planted in your cargo; (5) The caravan travels the Stormlands and uses unusual beasts to pull your wagons; (6) Your caravan has collected many myths and legends, making the wise elders respected sages; (7) The safety of the caravan has been ensured with an ancient pledge to the infernals; (8) A former member has joined a band of raiders and waits for revenge; (9) You carry a seal of friendship with the Rhulic dwarves for safe passage; (10) Since the Claiming, a debt owed to a merchant in Llael has been made null and void.

background.name: Glimmerwood Kriel
entries: {@b Adventuring Company.} Your company is a tribe that lives deep in the Glimmerwood. Here, within the dense forests and far from civilization, your tribe is able to live off the land and conduct life in the traditional way of your people. The Ryn consider the woods haunted, allowing the people of the woods to live in seclusion and peace.

entries.name: Prerequisites
entries: At least one member of a Glimmerwood Kriel company must be a trollkin. Other members ideally should be trollkin, or members of other races that claim kinship with the trollkin and make their homes within the woods.

entries.name: Company Accomplishments
entries: Your company's prestige increases through acts such as: driving off a rival tribe from your people's ancestral land; rediscovering a lost holy site or krielstone; slaying a dangerous beast; reclaiming an ancient Molgur relic worth at least 1,000 gp; binding a large, monstrous troll to your kriel.

entries.name: Tier 1 — Tribal Woodsmen
entries: {@b Encampment.} You have a well-built collection of shacks where you can rest, house your mounts, and store food, equipment, and livestock.

{@b The Kulgat Oath.} All members of your kriel are assumed to have formed around this blood ritual. You have advantage on ability checks related to social situations when you meet a stranger who is related by blood or oath to the kriel's trollkin.

{@b Voice of the Ancestors.} You can consult your company's krielstones and scrolls for lore regarding the local lands. You can replace one proficiency from your race or background with proficiency in your choice of the Religion skill or the History skill.

entries.name: Tier 2 — Clansmen
entries: {@b Shrine.} You can research the temple scrolls as a downtime activity (at least 8 hours). After this time, you can choose one 1st-level spell you know and replace it with another one from the appropriate spell list. Fell callers who engage in this downtime gain a bonus use of their Fell Call feature for the next 24 hours.
{@b Tribe.} You gain {@dice 1d4} hirelings willing to defend your home. Hirelings must have a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower.

entries.name: Tier 3 — Kriel Warriors
entries: {@b Kuar.} A druid who spends time here can replace one known spell of 3rd level or lower with another from the druid spell list and can produce a 1st-level spell scroll. Additionally, a character who spends {@dice 1d6} days of training and 50 gp of material can attempt an animus shaping on a warbeast.
{@b Warriors of the Land.} You gain {@dice 1d6} hirelings with a challenge rating of 1 or lower.

entries.name: Tier 4 — Legends Writ Upon the Stones
entries: {@b Masters of the Beasts.} Your company now breeds warbeasts. You can bond with one additional warbeast you share a resonance with.

{@b A Song of Stone and Thunder.} When interacting with others, you can replace Charisma (Persuasion) and Charisma (Intimidation) checks with Strength (Athletics) checks. In addition, you can perform a number of extra fell calls equal to your Strength modifier.

entries.name: Quirks
entries: Roll or choose a quirk: (1) Your settlement includes an ancient tree where rituals are made in the name of Dhunia; (2) The quitari of your kriel is that of an older, once-lost kriel to which you are related by blood; (3) Your settlement sits on a locus of ley lines suffused with energy; (4) A band of raiders is led by a former member of the kriel ousted due to a crime; (5) Your krielstone and scrolls belong to a more ancient Molgur tribe and tell of ancient wars; (6) Your settlement is renowned for the ale or spirits it brews; (7) Your settlement is a small offshoot of a larger kriel that you must respond to; (8) Your kriel is made up of members of several fallen kriels; (9) A strange beast stalks your territory; (10) Your settlement adheres to a strict code of honor and will never cheat others or kill those who surrender.

background.name: Occult Bushwhackers
entries: {@b Adventuring Company.} A band of bushwhackers is a collection of hunters, bone grinders, trappers, explorers, and mystics who learn all they can from the land and carve out a territory from which they gather resources to trade or fashion into weapons, alchemical serums, and fetishes.

entries.name: Prerequisites
entries: Any character can belong to an Occult Bushwhackers company, but it must include at least one bone grinder.

entries.name: Ritual Workings
entries: If you are an alchemist, you can make use of one formula or talisman that you don't currently know a number of times equal to your Intelligence modifier (minimum of once).

entries.name: Company Accomplishments
entries: Your company's prestige increases through acts such as: discovering an ancient Molgur relic or ritual site; capturing or killing a dangerous creature; fashioning a powerful fetish worth at least 1,500 gp; discovering a rare or exotic plant or creature and presenting it to a notable chieftain or lord; developing a powerful new alchemical formula or fetish.

entries.name: Tier 1 — Poachers
entries: {@b Camp.} You have access to regular hunting grounds, tents, a large cauldron for cooking and brewing, and a modest collection of alchemical ingredients. You can craft an extra alchemical item during a short or long rest.
{@b Hunting Grounds.} You can replace one proficiency from your race or background with proficiency in the Survival skill.

entries.name: Tier 2 — Skilled Hunters
entries: {@b Well-Stocked Provisions.} If you spend at least 8 hours in camp researching, you can choose one common formula you know and replace it with another, or choose one 1st-level spell you know and replace it with another from your spell list. After a long rest at your camp, you regain two uses of your field alchemy kit.
{@b Whipper-In and Kenneler.} You gain {@dice 1d8} hirelings who act as kennelers and whipper-ins. Hirelings must have a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower.

entries.name: Tier 3 — Big Game Hunters
entries: {@b Improved Provisions.} As a downtime activity, a member can choose one known uncommon formula/talisman and replace it with one uncommon formula/talisman or two common formulas/talismans. After a long rest at camp, you regain five uses of your field alchemy kit.
{@b Trophy Hunters.} You gain a +2 bonus to Charisma (Persuasion) checks when dealing with other alchemists and bone grinders.

entries.name: Tier 4 — Monster Slayers
entries: {@b Master Alchemists.} When interacting with others, you can replace Charisma (Intimidation) and Charisma (Persuasion) checks with Intelligence (Arcana) checks.

{@b Monster Slayers.} Once per turn, you can add your Wisdom modifier to your attack roll against a Large or larger creature. In addition, you have advantage on saving throws against being frightened by Large or larger creatures.

entries.name: Quirks
entries: Roll or choose a quirk: (1) Your company is a ragtag collection of hunters and bone grinders from other tribes; (2) Your company was founded after you defeated a savage beast that was raiding a village; (3) You were originally outriders and scouts for your tribe, but you returned to find many of your people dead; (4) Your company was formed by a notable patron who calls upon you at inopportune moments; (5) One of your founding members left after you discovered them stealing and poaching—they are now a sworn enemy; (6) Your company follows a peculiar tradition and wears a unique pendant from the time of the Molgur; (7) Your company is followed by a small annoying creature that steals items but acts as an early warning; (8) Your company has existed for decades, passing down hunting grounds through family bloodlines; (9) Your company was founded during a great ritual meal; (10) The fleeing skorne forces have left vast swathes of land unclaimed, creating prime hunting grounds.

background.name: Retribution Remnant
entries: {@b Adventuring Company.} The Claiming came and, with it, the end of your gods. With the road ahead uncertain and Ios now a kingdom of the dead ruled by eldritch, the only option was back out into the badlands and on to make some sort of life as soldiers with no kingdom or gods.

entries.name: Prerequisites
entries: All members are presumed to be Iosans, with most being former members of the Retribution of Scyrah, though such companies may have accepted members from other races and kingdoms.

entries.name: Company Accomplishments
entries: Your company's prestige increases through acts such as: recovering an Iosan myrmidon; recovering an Iosan cortex; finding some sign of the elven gods' return; defeating a major raiding party attacking Iosan refugees; negotiating a favorable mercenary contract.

entries.name: Tier 1 — Echoes of Scyrah
entries: {@b Arcanist Armorer.} You receive a 10 percent discount on costs to repair your weapons and gear with a trusted arcanist weaponsmith.

{@b Mercenary Charter.} Your company holds a mercenary charter allowing you to legally fight within the borders of the Iron Kingdoms. The charter details civilian treatment, loot distribution, and contract obligations. Contracts are worth no more than 200 gp at this tier.
{@b Sellswords.} You can go looking for contracts as a downtime activity. Gathering such information costs {@dice 1d4} gp per day for {@dice 1d4} days.

entries.name: Tier 2 — Shadows of Scyrah
entries: {@b Forgotten of Scyrah.} You gain your choice of {@dice 1d6} hirelings with a challenge rating of 1/2 or lower, or a myrmidon with a challenge rating of 4 or lower that will require time and money to bring up to full working order.

{@b Mementos of Scyrah.} An Iosan arcanist who finishes a long rest at camp and meditates in the presence of the relics can choose one spell of 1st or 2nd level and replace it with another from the warcaster spell list, or produce a spell engine that can generate {@dice 1d4} 2nd-level spells.

entries.name: Tier 3 — Wraiths of Scyrah
entries: {@b Eyes of Lacyr.} When you make a death saving throw at the start of your turn, you have advantage on the roll. You can't use this effect again until you finish a short or long rest.
{@b Trained Soldiers.} You gain {@dice 1d10} hirelings with a challenge rating of 1 or lower who act as privates and {@dice 1d4} hirelings with a challenge rating of 2 or lower who act as sergeants.

entries.name: Tier 4 — Remembrance of Scyrah
entries: {@b Beacon of Ios.} When interacting with others, you can replace Charisma (Persuasion) and Charisma (Intimidation) checks with Intelligence (Religion) checks.

{@b Militant Order.} You gain {@dice 1d10} hirelings with a challenge rating of 2 or lower and {@dice 1d4} hirelings with a challenge rating of 4 or lower. In place of half the hirelings, you can choose to gain a myrmidon with a challenge rating of 4 or lower.

entries.name: Quirks
entries: Roll or choose a quirk: (1) You have a few books from the city of Shyrr detailing operas you once saw there; (2) Your company is made up of members from numerous houses of Ios bound together as refugees; (3) You have skorne artifacts, including an oculus, in your collection of loot; (4) All members wear small slivers of white marble with blue veins cut from a stone from the Great Fane of Lacyr; (5) You have a patron who funds your company in return for loyalty and aid; (6) Your company resides in an Iosan outpost haunted by one of the riven; (7) The last of a noble bloodline died in battle leading some of your company—you have taken on their name; (8) Your company includes some Nyss elves and the two peoples exchange culture and expertise; (9) The loss of your gods has turned you toward Dhunia or the Wurm; (10) You have a contact within the borders of Ios and await the right time to claim a cache of weapons.

background.name: The Brand of Odom
entries: {@b Adventuring Company.} Through the centuries, the conclaves of the Brand of Odom have passed down their traditions, indoctrinating all Rhulic wizards into the Brand. Some of these arcanists are tasked with affairs of state, and venture forth to root out heretics, traitors, and arcane secrets.

entries.name: Prerequisites
entries: Members are citizens of Rhul, and at least one member should be a Rhulic arcanist who is a member of the Brand and follows the oaths and laws laid down by Great Father Odom.

entries.name: Company Accomplishments
entries: Your company's prestige increases through acts such as: negotiating a contract for rare minerals; discovering a unique magical device; discovering and destroying rogue Rhulic arcanists; fabricating a mechanikal item worth at least 1,500 gp; defeating an arcane threat to Rhul.

entries.name: Tier 1 — The Branded
entries: {@b Arcane Lodge.} You maintain a modest lodge with a common room, guest quarters, workshops, laboratories, a library, a kitchen, and stables. You can stay at the lodge and maintain a modest lifestyle at no expense. You gain a single hireling with a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower to act as the butler.

{@b Scholars of Magic.} You can replace one proficiency gained from your race or background with proficiency in the Arcana skill. If you are a spellcaster, you learn one additional cantrip from your spell lists.

entries.name: Tier 2 — Adepts of the Arcane
entries: {@b Arcane Forge.} As a downtime activity, you can produce {@dice 1d3} 1st-level spell engines or learn a new mechanika rune with a rune point cost of 2 or lower. The cost of crafting components for mechanikal items is reduced by 20 percent.

{@b Muscle and Metal.} You gain your choice of {@dice 1d6} hirelings with a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower, one Grundback Snapper steamjack with a challenge rating of 1 or lower, or an ogrun master-at-arms with a challenge rating of 1 or lower.

entries.name: Tier 3 — Mystics-at-Arms
entries: {@b Full Metal Arcanist.} As a downtime activity before a combat engagement, your company's spellcasters can choose any two spells of 2nd level or lower and replace them with two different spells, both primarily magical attacks. Non-spellcasters can spend downtime changing runeplates and charging capacitors.
{@b Law and Order.} When interacting with others, you can replace Wisdom (Insight) checks with Intelligence (Arcana) checks.

entries.name: Tier 4 — Elders of Odom
entries: {@b Company of Iron.} You gain your choice of {@dice 1d6} hirelings with a challenge rating of 1 or lower, or {@dice 1d4} ogrun with a challenge rating of 2 or lower.
{@b Foundry of Odom.} When crafting mechanikal items at your lodge, you receive a 30 percent discount on materials and reduce the time needed to craft by 20 percent.

entries.name: Quirks
entries: Roll or choose a quirk: (1) You have an anvil that dates back to the rebellion and the creation of the colossals; (2) You inherited the company after the passing of a friend; (3) You gained control of the lodge after winning an arcane duel—the former leader has left vowing revenge; (4) You discovered a vein of precious ore in a forgotten mine; (5) Your company has a long history with an ogrun tribe; (6) Your lodge sits upon a haunted mine from which spirits wander; (7) Your company was founded after you were witnesses in a trial and the defendant was executed; (8) Your lodge hides a secret: one of its founding members was a traitor; (9) Your lodge is an extension of a larger lodge; (10) Your lodge has the bones of some ancient beast rumored to have been slain by a legendary master of the Brand of Odom.

backgroundFluff.name: Barber-Surgeon
entries: People on the borderlands are hardy, and many are subject to life in harsh conditions. The ability to withstand brutal injuries and deadly environments means that some in the borderlands, particularly among the farrow, have an approach to medicine that is pragmatic—but not delicate. Barber- surgeons, with some glee, saw off limbs, drill holes in skulls to relive hematomas, and use medications that are almost as deadly as the injuries and diseases they are trying to cure. Amongst the trollkin, such surgeons are common, thanks to the regenerative ability of the trollkin race. Limb removal and invasive procedures can be performed without the worry of the patient dying. Amongst the human populations of the Iron Kingdoms, barber-surgeons are also common and considered distinct from doctors. While a doctor has studied at a university and practiced their profession on cadavers, barber-surgeons learn out in the field, acting as apprentices to their mentors, holding down patients as they watch a bone saw driven through yet another limb. Barber-surgeons are likely to be found amongst the itinerant human populations, villages, and military forces and onboard ships.

backgroundFluff.name: Desert Guide
entries: Some, such as the Idrians, various tribes of desert-dwelling bogrin, and trollkin, know the Bloodstone Marches intimately. These people understand every track, trail, and clue that is left upon the desert sands. Those with such skills travel far and wide and live for extended periods in the desert, where others would die of thirst in a matter of days, thus making them desirable as guides for caravans and armies traversing the scorching plains.

backgroundFluff.name: Fennblade
entries: Marching forward, swinging massive cleaving blades in wide arcs, Fennblades are a force of nature that hacks through soldiers and warjacks alike. Wielding hooked greatswords, Fennblades are mighty warriors of the kriels who often stand ready at the front of battle lines, prepared to take the brunt of a charge, even from thunderous heavy cavalry. The origin of the Fennblade fighting art comes from the First Trollkin War, when the kriels of the Fenn Marsh banded together bearing their brutal hooked swords. And while the skill to fight with such swords takes many hours of training, the fighting art has been adopted by kriels across western Immoren.

backgroundFluff.name: Guttersnipe
entries: Living within the cities of the Iron Kingdoms can be difficult for those outside the dominant culture. This is particularly true for gobbers and some of the bogrin who try to make a living in these cities. Prejudice and outright hostility push these outsiders to the cities’ fringes, where they eke out a life amongst the vagrants or form small collectives in the ghettos and undercities of these metropolises. Unwanted for respectable jobs, many enter a life of crime, and, in the case of gobbers, their natural aptitude for stealth and dexterity makes them perfect purse-snatchers and pickpockets. Guttersnipes refine these talents to excel at burglary, espionage, and shadowing others. Many can even anticipate the needs of their buyers, acquiring information and items before others even know they need what the guttersnipe has just stolen.

backgroundFluff.name: Northkin Fire Eaters
entries: Trollkin are renowned for the alcohol they brew, some of which have such high alcohol content or contain narcotic herbs that they would prove deadly to many of the peoples of western Immoren. Those trollkin who make their homes far to the north of Khador, deep within the snow-blanketed forests, ferment potent liquors that warm the body and chase away the chills. These trollkin both drink this alcohol and weaponize it, spitting gouts of the volatile liquids at the enemies while igniting it. Such fire-breathing skills make fire eaters formidable opponents as well as popular performers in other kriels.

backgroundFluff.name: Searforge Trader
entries: Throughout western Immoren, some of the finest weapons and sturdiest armor can be bought from Rhulic traders. This market for high-quality goods has allowed the Searforge Commission to make vast sums of money by trading with the human kingdoms, and they have invested that profit wisely in the manufacture of arms, steamjacks, and research into new alloys and alchemical explosives. These traders are savvy merchants with an eye for details and minds for politics, since what they sell can turn the tide of a battle elsewhere in the kingdoms. While traditionally only Rhulfolk were welcome among the Searforge Traders, others now act in this role since the advent of the freeholds.

backgroundFluff.name: Stormfall Archer
entries: Archery is not just a skill for hunting. The powerful bows used by the Iosan Stormfall archers launch arrows high into the air. When the arrows return, they detonate on impact. Such archers combine the destructive might of cannons and rockets with the mobility of skirmishers. These fast-moving troops can quickly redeploy and pepper the enemy lines with explosions, sowing discord through the ranks.

backgroundFluff.name: Urban Nomad
entries: Like the Nyss, the Iosans are a people without a homeland. Both peoples are on the brink of extinction, but some have found a life within the Iron Kingdoms and the cities of Rhul. Living within the cities of the Iron Kingdoms requires these nomads to adjust to their new lives and environs, as they cope with culture shock, exclusion, and distrust. However, nomads are often people with particular skills and knowledge that are rare to find within the Iron Kingdoms, and so nomads find easy employment in gangs, local militia, and the offices of private investigators.

backgroundFluff.name: Wasteland Commando
entries: Among the Farrow raiding parties, skorne remnants, and Idrian tribes of the Bloodstone Marches are a breed of cunning and murderous commandos. Unlike common brigands, they tend to be armed with various weapons taken as spoils on the field of battle. Furthermore, these commandos are not just mindless savages or opportunistic bandits but are calculating tacticians, planning attacks deep behind enemy lines or laying punishing ambushes.

backgroundFluff.name: Badlands Raiders
entries: A traveling caravan of traders has much to fear in their journeys as they plow a course from settlement to settlement. Out in the wilderness, roving bands of raiders prey on the weak, the foolhardy, and the naive. These brutal parties of opportunistic marauders stalk their quarry and launch surprise attacks that use the landscape to their advantage. Some raiding parties travel on steeds, riding animals of all varieties. Druidic peoples and their Tharn allies burst forth from the forests armed with bow and spear and mounted on slavering duskwolves. Farrow emerge from well-hidden bolt-holes, blasting crude firearms and riding grunting razor boars. From a distance, Nyss hunters assail their targets with arrows and blasts of arcane energies. Each company of raiders uses different methods, which they have refined over many escapades. Their motivations also differ: some attack to protect their traditional lands while others do so for money. Some raiders may even just do it for glory and pride.

entries.name: Prerequisites
entries: Any character can belong to a Badlands Raiders company, but it must include at least one character proficient in Survival.

entries.name: This Is a Stick Up
entries: Caravans and traders make for worthwhile sport and even worthier targets, as do scouts from rival tribes. As a downtime activity, any number of members from your company can set out to ambush travelers and locals on the roads. Each member of your company that goes out raiding chooses and rolls a d4, d6, d8, or d10. Each roll of 1 and each roll that is greater than the character’s Wisdom modifier + proficiency bonus is considered a failure. The company gains 10 gp × the sum of the successful rolls. If the total number of failures is greater than the maximum Wisdom modifier + proficiency bonus among all the company’s members, the raid results in a conflict with a well-armed force consisting of 3d4 creatures with a challenge rating of 1. These creatures can be an immediate encounter, as guards, or can arrive at a later inconvenient time, at the GM’s discretion.

entries.name: Company Accomplishments
entries: Your company’s prestige increases whenever you carve out your hunting territory and prove your ability through acts such as the following: • Hijacking a caravan, train, or steamboat • Destroying a rival tribe or company of raiders • Breaking someone out of prison • Stealing an item worth at least 1,000 gp • Capturing and selling 1,000 gp worth of livestock TIER 1: CATTLE RUSTLERS At this tier, your company is a small number of armed warriors who make a living raiding farms and small holdings, along with any small group of traveling merchants. At this tier, your marks are usually farmers and caravans with herds or goods with a total worth of no more than 100 gp. You might operate as a small party of some larger group of bandits or outriders for a tribe.

entries.name: Benefits
entries: Your company gains the following benefits at this tier. Hideout. You maintain a small camp somewhere in the scrubland or in a cave in the shadow of the mountains. Here you tend to whatever steeds and animals you have captured. You have tents for sleeping and a place where you stash your ill-gotten gains. Lay of the Land. You can scout the lands around you and gather information from other locals and travelers about potential marks. You might gain the information through small bribes or while drinking at taverns and villages. Spending downtime to gather such information requires an expenditure of 1d4 gp, which goes toward bribes and drinks. Gathering information takes 1d4 days. Martial Hand Signals. You can learn martial hand signals, which allows you and other members of your company to communicate silently, thereby allowing you to plan attacks without revealing your position via speech. TIER 2: HIGHWAY BANDITS At this tier, your company is a small number of armed warriors who make a living raiding farms and small holdings, along with any small group of traveling merchants. You might operate as a small party of some larger group of bandits or outriders for a tribe.

entries.name: Benefits
entries: Your company gains the following benefits at this tier. Bandits. Novices and new recruits seek out your group to learn your ways and make a life for themselves. You gain 1d4 hirelings with a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower. Improved Steeds. Your company has invested in its steeds and mounts, improving both their quality and their tack. While you are riding your steed, attack rolls targeting your mount are made with disadvantage. Loyal Informants. Your network of contacts throughout the lands is such that you receive tips on valuable cargo being transported by caravans and on steamboats. You now have leads on targets carrying 200 gp of valuables or the movement of herds of an equivalent value. Your awareness of the movements of the groups allows you to plan better ambushes.

TIER 3: FEARED RAIDERS Your band of raiders is now feared for many leagues around and spoken about in hushed tones in taverns and towns. Other bandits fear you and stay out of your territory, and tribal leaders come to you when they need extra muscle.

entries.name: Benefits
entries: Your company gains the following benefits at this tier. Additional Camps. Your company contains no fools and therefore maintains 1d4 extra campsites where supplies are stashed in case you need to flee your regular camp. Bandit Kings. Your company gains 2d8 hirelings with a challenge rating of 1/2 or lower. Half these new recruits have mounts with a challenge rating of 1/4 or lower. Battle Steeds. Your steeds and mounts have been trained to deal lethal attacks with their hooves, claws, and tusks. As a bonus action, you can direct your mount to make one attack with one of its weapons. TIER 4: LORDS OF

entries.name: The Badlands
entries: By the time your company has reached this tier, it has become renowned among raiders, feared by travelers and merchants,

backgroundFluff.name: The Brand of Odom
entries: The only official arcane organization in Rhul is also the oldest in all of western Immoren: the ancient and secretive Brand of Odom. Founded in prehistory by the Great Father whose name it shares, the Brand of Odom is unique among arcane orders in that its oaths and bylaws were laid down by divine edict rather than formulated by mortal minds. As such, the Brand of Odom holds an equally unique position in Rhulic society, with several highly placed members serving the government of Rhul as Moot Judges and advisors to clan lords. The organization’s knowledge and wisdom are highly valued by the Moot of the Hundred Houses, and even though members of the order do not serve on the Moot itself, they are often called upon to give testimony and offer insights. Furthermore, Rhul’s intelligence services rely as heavily on the Brand of Odom as they do on more traditional methods of gathering information, and the organization’s tactical arcanists are regularly seen alongside Rhulic soldiers. Originally an organization of wizards like the Order of Wizardry in Ceryl to the south, the Brand of Odom strives to maintain what essentially amounts to a monopoly on arcane lore within Rhul. To this end, it inducts arcane mechaniks and dwarven sorcerers into its ranks as well, although such individuals have rarely reached the highest echelons of the order. The organization’s strict rules are part of the edicts laid down by the Great Fathers in ancient times and are thus virtually impossible to change, which has caused the Brand of Odom to lag behind in certain areas of study. These include necromancy, which remains forbidden by the edicts even as it gains greater—if grudging—acceptance across the Iron Kingdoms. Strict oaths of secrecy and loyalty bind the members of the Brand of Odom and restrict them from teaching their arts to outsiders, but nothing prevents a dwarf beyond the borders of Rhul from learning arcane secrets in other ways, and more than one aspiring dwarven arcanist has fled Rhul in order to study secrets forbidden by the order. The organization grants its members considerable leeway to pursue their own agendas, but all are expected to answer the call whenever a senior member of the order or the Moot of the Hundred Houses requires their service. Outsiders are often surprised to learn that the order’s name is quite literal. Upon advancing beyond the rank of apprentice and being fully inducted into the order, each member of the Brand of Odom is marked with an ancient dwarven rune associated with Great Father Odom. Aside from serving as a way of identifying other members of the order, the rune, which roughly translates to “that which is hidden,” is enchanted when administered and can be used by high-ranking members to locate a Brand of Odom wizard at any time, regardless of the wizard’s location on Caen.

entries.name: The Church of the Great Fathers
entries: Even though the Rhulfolk are not as aggressively devout as the citizens of the now-depopulated Protectorate of Menoth to the south, worship of the Great Fathers and the Claywives is as inextricably tied to the politics of Rhul as veneration of the Lawgiver is to the Protectorate. Because the Great Fathers handed down the edicts that became the basis of the Codex, which still governs all laws throughout Rhul, the church and politics have always gone hand-in-glove in the dwarven nation. Unlike the Protectorate, Rhul never developed into a theocracy; instead, its clergy hold a vital advisory role at virtually every level of government, and the justicars of the Church of the Great Fathers are regularly seen in public courts throughout Rhul, advising Moot Judges and mediating conflicts between clans or even individuals. The Claywives were the first clerics of the Church of the Great Fathers, so it is hardly surprising that female dwarves dominated both the clergy and the judiciary for centuries. Over time, however, the balance of power within the church has shifted, and all genders are now represented at every level of power. There are even ogrun and, more rarely, other peoples who have joined the clergy of the Great Fathers, although their shorter lifespans make them less likely to attain the highest ranks within the church. Unlike the Moot of the Hundred Houses, which operates out of the Moot Hall, the Church of the Great Fathers does not have a specific headquarters. Most consider the Library of the Codex in Ghord the most sacred building in Rhul, for it is where the original edicts are still kept, along with records of every moot for the last six millennia. This also makes it one of the largest—and certainly the oldest—repositories of knowledge and history in all of western Immoren. The Library of the Codex is home to numerous senior members of the clergy at any given time, and countless scribes work diligently within its halls to preserve, copy, and restore antique texts. Even so, it is no more the operating center of the church than any of the other temples to the Great Fathers that are found in every Rhulic city. It is, however, an excellent illustration of the way in which other aspects of dwarven life are integrated into the church and vice versa. Despite being the church’s domain, the library is guarded by members of the Rhulic military and protected by magical wards and spells placed there by the wizards of the Brand of Odom. These safeguards help ensure the safety of the dwarven sages, scholars, bureaucrats, and lawmakers who frequently make use of the extensive records kept within the library’s walls. The church’s intimate integration into Rhulic life extends

far beyond the highest ranks of government. Every day, dwarves bring their problems and grievances to local members of the clergy, who often offer advice and mediate conflicts long before they require the intervention of the judiciary. Furthermore, the church acts as a safe haven in times of interclan feuds and other strife, offering sanctuary to any Rhulfolk who seek it—and woe to any dwarf foolish or impious enough to visit violence upon one of these temples or on the heads of those who have taken shelter within them.

entries.name: The Glomring
entries: Those who hail from outside Rhul often struggle to understand the importance of clan in dwarven society. As such, they also have difficulty fully comprehending the complex, precarious, and unusual role played by the Glomring, an organization of spies and smugglers who operate both within and adjacent to Rhulic law. monster.name: Ghordson Basher
languages: understands the languages of its manufacturer but can't speak

trait.name: Cortex
entries: The Ghordson Basher's cortex allows it to understand basic commands from its controller (spoken verbally by most but usually communicated telepathically by warcasters). Verbal commands must be akin to those issued to a trained animal, such as "stay," "guard," "attack," and so on. The Ghordson Basher will carry out these commands with no regard for its own safety.

trait.name: Charge
entries: If the Ghordson Basher moves at least 20 feet straight toward a creature and then hits it with a slam attack on the same turn, the target takes an extra 9 ({@damage 2d8}) bludgeoning damage.

trait.name: Heavy Metal
entries: The Ghordson Basher's attacks are magical for the purpose of overcoming resistances and immunity to nonmagical attacks for steamjacks, colossals, and warbeasts.

trait.name: Steam Powered
entries: The Ghordson Basher requires coal and water to function. When not in combat, it can function for 13 hours with a full fuel load of 900 pounds of coal and fresh water in its boiler. While in combat, the Ghordson Basher can function for 2 hours with a full fuel load. If the Ghordson Basher's coal and water are not refilled at the end of this time, it suffers one level of exhaustion at the end of each minute. Due to the amount of noise its steam engine produces, the Ghordson Basher has disadvantage on Dexterity (Stealth) checks.

trait.name: Durable
entries: When this Rhulic steamjack suffers catastrophic damage, a player can roll twice and choose which catastrophic damage result to apply.

trait.name: Sturdy
entries: Due to its low center of gravity and bulky frame, this Rhulic steamjack cannot be pushed by nonmagical means.

entries: The Ghordson Basher makes two slam attacks.

entries: While within 30 feet of the officer, each High Shield gunner who can hear and understand the officer can make two attacks each time it makes a ranged attack.

trait.name: Hold the Line
entries: The Forge Guard gains a +1 bonus to AC while standing within 5 feet of an ally.

trait.name: Tempered by War
entries: The Forge Guard has advantage on saving throws against being frightened.

action.name: Mechanikal Hammer
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}10 ({@damage 2d6+3}) bludgeoning damage. If the target is a Medium or smaller creature, it must succeed on a DC 15 Strength saving throw or be knocked prone.

monster.name: Ogrun Bokur
languages: Rhulic, Molgur-Og, and any one language

trait.name: Charge
entries: If the ogrun moves at least 20 feet straight toward a target and then hits it with a melee weapon attack on the same turn, the target takes an extra 3 ({@damage 1d6}) damage.

trait.name: Client
entries: The ogrun has sworn an oath to guard a particular client. So long as the ogrun's client is alive, not incapacitated, and within the bokur's line of sight, the ogrun makes attack rolls and saving throws with advantage.

trait.name: Second Wind (Recharges after a Short or Long Rest)
entries: The ogrun can use a bonus action to regain 10 hit points.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The ogrun makes two attacks: one with its warcleaver and one with its shield bash.

action.name: Ogrun Warcleaver
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}9 ({@damage 1d12+3}) slashing damage.

action.name: Shield Bash
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}6 ({@damage 1d6+3}) bludgeoning damage.

reaction.name: Shield Guard
entries: When a creature makes an attack against the ogrun's client, the ogrun grants a +2 bonus to the client's AC if the ogrun is within 5 feet of the client.

trait.name: Battle Wizard
entries: When the arcanist reduces a creature to 0 hit points, the arcanist can use its reaction to cast a spell with a casting time of 1 action or 1 bonus action.

trait.name: Destructive Synchronicity
entries: The arcanist has advantage on attack rolls against any creature that has taken damage this round.

trait.name: Magic Weapon
entries: The arcanist's thermo mace attacks are magical.

trait.name: Steam Armor
entries: The arcanist's standard steam armor provides it with a damage threshold of 10; attacks that deal less than 10 damage do not damage the arcanist. The armor requires a load of 10 pounds of coal and 5 gallons of water for 5 hours of general use or 1 hour of combat. If the armor does not have power, the arcanist's base speed becomes 15.

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: The arcanist is a 5th-level spellcaster. Its spellcasting ability is Intelligence (spell save DC 13, {@hit 5} to hit with spell attacks). It has the following spells prepared:

action.name: Thermo Mace
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}6 ({@damage 1d8+2}) bludgeoning damage plus 3 ({@damage 1d6}) fire damage.

trait.name: Hardy
entries: The bison has advantage on saving throws against effects that cause exhaustion.

trait.name: Charge
entries: If the bison moves at least 20 feet straight toward a target and then hits it with a gore attack on the same turn, the target takes an extra 5 ({@damage 2d4}) piercing damage. If the target is a creature, it must succeed on a DC 13 Strength saving throw or be knocked prone.

trait.name: Charge

trait.name: Sure-Footed
entries: The goat has advantage on Strength and Dexterity saving throws made against effects that would knock it prone.

action.name: Ram
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}10 ({@damage 2d6+3}) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Hooves
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 5 ft., one prone creature. {@h}13 ({@damage 4d4+3}) bludgeoning damage.

trait.name: Charge
entries: If the ulk moves at least 20 feet straight toward a target and then hits it with a ram attack on the same turn, the target takes an extra 7 ({@damage 2d6}) bludgeoning damage. If the target is a creature, it must succeed on a DC 13 Strength saving throw or be knocked prone.

trait.name: Ice Walk
entries: The ulk can move across and climb icy surfaces without needing to make an ability check. Additionally, difficult terrain composed of ice or snow doesn't cost it extra movement.
